mission.title: Install and Configure Valkey
mission.description: Valkey is a high-performance distributed key-value database optimized for caching and real-time workloads. It is suitable for applications requiring fast data access and supports various data structures and plugins. Key trade-offs include the need for careful configuration and potential complexity in deployment.

steps.title: Add Valkey Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Valkey Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add valkey https://charts.valkey.io

```

> This is the official repository for Valkey Helm charts.

steps.title: Install Valkey CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions required for Valkey to function properly.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/valkey-io/valkey/main/deploy/crds/valkey_crd.yaml

```

> Ensure CRDs are installed before deploying the application.

steps.title: Install Valkey using Helm
steps.description:
This command installs Valkey in the specified namespace with the desired version.

```bash

helm install valkey valkey/valkey --namespace valkey --version 9.0.3

```

> Replace 'valkey' with your desired release name.

steps.title: Create Valkey Custom Resource
steps.description:
This step creates a Valkey instance using a Custom Resource definition.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: valkey.io/v1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: my-valkey
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

```

> This creates a Valkey instance with 3 replicas.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: valkey.io/v1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: my-valkey
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

steps.title: Verify Valkey Deployment
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the Valkey pods to ensure they are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n valkey

```

> You should see all Valkey pods in the Running state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Valkey
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Valkey deployment and associated resources. Be cautious as this may lead to data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall valkey --namespace valkey

kubectl delete crd valkeys.valkey.io

```

> This command will remove the Valkey release.
> This will remove the Custom Resource Definitions associated with Valkey.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Valkey
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your data. This process includes draining the pods, upgrading, and verifying the installation.

```bash

kubectl drain <pod-name> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade valkey valkey/valkey --namespace valkey --version 9.0.3

kubectl get pods -n valkey

```

> Replace <pod-name> with the name of your Valkey pod.
> This command upgrades Valkey to the specified version.
> Verify that all pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Valkey server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Valkey server may not be running or is unreachable.

**Fix:**
Check the status of the Valkey pods with 'kubectl get pods -n valkey' and ensure they are running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Valkey instance not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified Valkey instance may not exist or was deleted.

**Fix:**
Verify the existence of the Valkey instance with 'kubectl get valkeys -n valkey'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources to schedule pod'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Kubernetes cluster may not have enough resources available.

**Fix:**
Check the resource allocation and consider scaling your cluster or adjusting resource requests.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Permission denied'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** RBAC settings may be preventing access to the Valkey resources.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the correct RBAC roles and bindings are set up for your user or service account.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for new data structures and improved plugin system.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated older APIs for data access.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from version 8.x, ensure to update your configurations to align with the new API changes.

resolution.summary: You should see the Valkey pods running with the output similar to 'my-valkey-<hash>  1/1     Running   0          5m'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: valkey.io/v1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: my-valkey
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster up and running with the required tools installed.
